title: althaea officinalis
intro: Althaea officinalis, also known as marshmallow plant or marsh mallow, is a perennial plant native to Europe, Western Asia, and North Africa, belonging to the mallow family (Malvaceae). The roots and leaves of this plant have been used in traditional medicine for their soothing and anti-inflammatory properties, making it a popular remedy for digestive issues, skin irritations, and respiratory problems. The medicinal properties of Althaea officinalis are attributed to its high mucilage content, which provides protective and lubricating effects on the mucous membranes, promoting health benefits such as wound healing, reduced inflammation, and improved digestion. Althaea officinalis can be prepared into various herbal products, including teas, tinctures, creams, and ointments, which are often used to treat skin conditions, coughs, and sore throats.
intro_study: According to Plant disease, Althaea officinalis has not been mentioned to have medicinal purposes in the given study, as it primarily discusses the foliar disease caused by Colletotrichum malvarum on the plant.
benefits_desc: Althaea officinalis is used to alleviate a range of health issues, from digestive problems such as irritable bowel syndrome and inflammatory bowel disease, to skin conditions like eczema and acne. Its medicinal uses also extend to soothing sore throats, reducing coughs, and providing relief from menstrual cramps and other gynecological issues. Additionally, Althaea officinalis has been shown to have numerous health benefits, including reducing inflammation, improving wound healing, and even helping to manage diabetes and high blood pressure. By promoting healthy digestion, reducing stress, and providing antioxidant benefits, this plant has become a valuable natural remedy for maintaining overall well-being.

benefits_list.name: anti-inflammatory properties
benefits_list.desc: althaea officinalis contains mucilages that provide anti-inflammatory properties, helping to soothe and calm irritated tissues.

benefits_list.desc: the plant's mucilages and other compounds help to soothe and calm skin irritations, such as eczema, acne, and minor cuts and scrapes.

benefits_list.desc: althaea officinalis has been traditionally used to soothe digestive issues, such as ulcers, inflammation, and irritable bowel syndrome.

benefits_list.desc: the plant contains compounds with antibacterial and antifungal properties, helping to prevent infections and promote wound healing.

benefits_list.name: expectorant properties
benefits_list.desc: althaea officinalis has been traditionally used as an expectorant to help relieve coughs and congestion, making it easier to breathe.

benefits_list.name: gastroprotective properties
benefits_list.desc: the plant's mucilages and other compounds help to protect the stomach lining, reducing inflammation and preventing ulcers.

benefits_list.name: wound healing
benefits_list.desc: althaea officinalis has been traditionally used to promote wound healing, due to its antibacterial, antifungal, and anti-inflammatory properties.

actions_desc: Althaea officinalis has demonstrated various therapeutic actions, including anti-inflammatory, demulcent, soothing, and mucilaginous properties, which primarily affect the mucous membranes, gastrointestinal tract, and respiratory system. The plant's demulcent action helps to form a protective barrier on the mucous membranes, reducing irritation and inflammation. Additionally, its soothing and mucilaginous properties exhibit anti-inflammatory, anti-irritant, and emollient actions, which benefit the skin, eyes, and respiratory system. Furthermore, the plant's therapeutic actions also include antioxidant, vulnerary, and immunomodulatory properties, which impact the overall health and well-being of the body.

actions_list.desc: creates a barrier on the skin's surface to protect it from environmental stressors and damage.

constituents_desc: Althaea officinalis has been found to contain a rich array of bioactive constituents, including flavonoids such as kaempferol and quercetin, which exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The presence of tannins, like gallic acid and catechins, contributes to the plant's astringent and antimicrobial effects. Additionally, the bioactive constituents of Althaea officinalis include mucilages, like galactomannans and glycoproteins, which have soothing and protective properties for mucous membranes. The plant also contains saponins, such as hederagenin and oleanolic acid, which possess anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects.

parts_desc: The most commonly used parts of Althaea officinalis are the roots, leaves, flowers, and seeds. The roots are primarily used in traditional medicine to treat digestive issues, such as ulcers and gastritis, and are also employed to soothe respiratory problems like bronchitis and coughs. The leaves and flowers are used in herbal teas and infusions to treat various conditions, including fever, sore throats, and skin irritations, while the seeds are used in some herbal remedies to treat issues like insomnia and anxiety. Additionally, the mucilaginous properties of the plant make it a popular ingredient in topical creams and ointments for skin conditions like eczema and dermatitis.

parts_list.name: root
parts_list.desc: the root of althaea officinalis is used in herbal medicine for its demulcent properties, which help to soothe and protect irritated mucous membranes.

parts_list.name: leaves
parts_list.desc: the leaves of the plant are smaller and less potent than the roots, but can still be used in herbal remedies for their anti-inflammatory properties.

parts_list.name: flowers
parts_list.desc: the flowers of althaea officinalis are small and star-shaped, and are used in herbal medicine to make teas and infusions that aid in digestion and soothe the stomach.

parts_list.name: stems
parts_list.desc: the stems of the plant are fibrous and can be used to make a medicinal tea or infusion that helps to soothe and calm the digestive system.

parts_list.name: petioles
parts_list.desc: the petioles of althaea officinalis are the stalks that attach the leaves to the stem, and are used in herbal medicine to make teas and infusions that aid in digestion and soothe the stomach.

parts_list.desc: the rhizomes of the plant are underground stems that produce new shoots and roots, and are used in herbal medicine to make teas and infusions that aid in digestion and soothe the stomach.

parts_list.name: cortex
parts_list.desc: the cortex of althaea officinalis refers to the outer layer of the root, which is used in herbal medicine to make teas and infusions that soothe and protect irritated mucous membranes.

preparations_desc: Althaea officinalis, also known as marshmallow, is a plant rich in mucilages that have been used for centuries in herbal medicine. The most common herbal preparations of Althaea officinalis include infusions, decoctions, tinctures, and topical creams. Infusions and decoctions are typically used to soothe digestive issues, such as irritable bowel syndrome, and to calm coughs and sore throats, while also being employed to treat skin conditions like eczema and acne. Tinctures and topical creams are often used to reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms of conditions like arthritis, while also being applied to wounds and burns to promote healing and reduce scarring.

preparations_list.name: infusion
preparations_list.desc: a liquid herbal preparation made by steeping the leaves and roots of the althaea officinalis plant in hot water.

preparations_list.name: tincture
preparations_list.desc: a concentrated liquid herbal preparation made by soaking the leaves and roots of the althaea officinalis plant in a solvent, often ethanol.

preparations_list.name: decoction
preparations_list.desc: a liquid herbal preparation made by boiling the roots of the althaea officinalis plant in water.

preparations_list.name: infused oil
preparations_list.desc: a topical herbal preparation made by infusing the leaves and roots of the althaea officinalis plant in a carrier oil.

preparations_list.name: salve
preparations_list.desc: a topical herbal preparation made by infusing the leaves and roots of the althaea officinalis plant in a carrier oil and then mixing with a wax.

preparations_list.name: glycoside extract
preparations_list.desc: a concentrated herbal preparation made by extracting the glycosides from the leaves and roots of the althaea officinalis plant.

preparations_list.name: powder
preparations_list.desc: a dry herbal preparation made by grinding the leaves and roots of the althaea officinalis plant into a fine powder.

side_effects_desc: Althaea officinalis side effects are generally mild and temporary, but may include nausea, dizziness, stomach cramps, diarrhea, vomiting, and allergic reactions such as hives and itching in some individuals. People with sensitive stomachs or those taking medications like blood thinners, diabetes medications, or blood pressure medications are most likely to experience these side effects. Precautions also include being cautious with the plant if you have kidney or liver problems, as it may exacerbate these conditions. Additionally, pregnant or breastfeeding women should use althaea officinalis with caution, as it may stimulate the uterus or cause allergic reactions in their infants.

side_effects_list.name: gastrointestinal upset
side_effects_list.desc: ingestion of althaea officinalis may cause stomach discomfort, nausea, and vomiting, especially when consumed in large quantities or in combination with other medications.

side_effects_list.name: allergic reactions
side_effects_list.desc: some individuals may experience allergic reactions, including skin rashes, itching, and swelling, after exposure to althaea officinalis.

side_effects_list.name: interactions with medications
side_effects_list.desc: althaea officinalal may interact with certain medications, such as blood thinners, diabetes medications, and blood pressure medications, which can lead to adverse effects or reduced efficacy.

side_effects_list.name: hypersensitivity to pollen
side_effects_list.desc: as althaea officinalis belongs to the malvaceae family, individuals with hypersensitivity to pollen from this family may experience symptoms such as sneezing, runny nose, and itchy eyes.

side_effects_list.name: diarrhea
side_effects_list.desc: consumption of althaea officinalis may cause diarrhea in some individuals, especially when consumed in large quantities or in combination with other laxative herbs.

side_effects_list.name: headache
side_effects_list.desc: ingestion of althaea officinalis may cause headache in some individuals, possibly due to the plant's diuretic and vasodilatory effects.

side_effects_list.name: dizziness
side_effects_list.desc: consumption of althaea officinalis may cause dizziness or lightheadedness in some individuals, especially when standing up quickly after sitting or lying down.
